title: Near-deterministic single-atom loading on a photonic integrated circuit
authors: Xinchao Zhou, Ahreum Lee, Dipanjan Das, Saivirinchi Prabandhakavi, Chen-Lung Hung
abstract: Coupling identical quantum emitters to a photonic integrated circuit (PIC) is a key step for scaling up emitter-photon interfaces for quantum science and information processing. Neutral atoms are attractive candidates due to their indistinguishability and controllability. However, experimental realizations of efficient atom trapping on a PIC while achieving strong single atom-photon coupling has so-far remained elusive. Here, we demonstrate near-deterministic single-atom loading on a microring resonator circuit, reaching single-atom cooperativity parameter C > 1 for strong coupling in cavity quantum electrodynamics. We utilize a precision optical conveyor belt, formed by a moving optical lattice in an optical tweezer, to steadily deliver trapped atoms onto a PIC. By continuously monitoring the transmission of probe photons through the circuit, which is sensitive to the proximity of single atoms near a microring resonator, we detect mean occupancy of 1.5 from 70 occupied lattice sites in a conveyor-belt transport of 4 nm position reproducibility. Based upon real-time feedback, we deterministically transfer the delivered atoms into a stationary trap on the microring, achieving 82% (18%) probability of single-(two-)atom transfer. Our technique can be extended to deterministic, highly efficient atom array assembly, providing a scalable route for neutral atom integration with PICs of complex functionalities.
summary: The research presents a novel method using an optical conveyor belt and real-time feedback to load single neutral atoms near a microring resonator on a PIC. This technique successfully achieves the strong coupling regime ($C>1$) and demonstrates deterministic loading, paving the way for scalable quantum information processing architectures.
main_problem: The primary challenge is achieving efficient, scalable integration of identical quantum emitters (neutral atoms) with Photonic Integrated Circuits (PICs) while maintaining strong single atom-photon coupling.
main_result: The authors demonstrated near-deterministic single-atom loading onto a microring resonator PIC, successfully reaching the strong coupling regime ($C > 1$).
method: They employed an optical conveyor belt formed by a moving optical lattice to continuously deliver atoms, followed by real-time feedback control for deterministic trapping into a stationary site.
model_or_system: The system involves Cesium neutral atoms coupled to microring resonators on a silicon-nitride PIC. The coupling is mediated by the atom's interaction with the cavity mode, monitored via atom-induced transparency changes in transmitted probe photons.
key_observables: Single-atom cooperativity parameter ($C$), single/two-atom transfer probabilities ($P_1 \approx 82\%$, $P_2 \approx 18\%$), and photon antibunching ($g^{(2)}(0) < 0.5$).
important_parameters: Strong coupling regime ($C>1$); position resolution down to 4 nm; mean occupancy $ ar{n} \approx 1.2$.
main_assumptions: The reduced two-atom probability is hypothesized to be due to inelastic collision loss during trap transfer and probing.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the setup schematic, the conveyor belt transport process, measured transmission spectra showing atom presence, and feedback control loops using APD monitoring.
paper_structure: The paper details the experimental realization of an optical conveyor belt for controlled atomic delivery, followed by active feedback implementation to achieve deterministic loading into a stationary trap on the PIC. Characterization includes measuring coupling strength and confirming single-atom emission via photon correlation measurements.
why_interesting: This work provides a crucial experimental blueprint for scaling up quantum devices by integrating neutral atoms deterministically onto solid-state photonic platforms, which is vital for realizing large-scale quantum simulators or processors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Conveyor-belt transport from a cold atom reservoir. a, Schematic of the experimental setup. An optical conveyor belt, formed by a moving optical lattice in an optical tweezer, transports a steady stream of trapped atoms onto a microring resonator. The microring draws in resonant photons from a bus waveguide. Atom-induced transparency in cavity QED generates light pulses synchronized with the conveyor-belt movement. Inset shows a scanning electron micrograph of the microring array. b, Experimental procedure depicting the initial atom guiding (drop), loading into an optical lattice (localize) with additional cooling (green arrow), and the subsequent conveyor-belt transport (convey)...

FIG. 3. Site-resolved measurement. a, Averaged trans- mission T of 70 lattice sites in one transport. Shaded band shows the fitted transmission curve and its uncertainty, based on the model Tn in Fig. 2c and weighted averaged using Pois- son atom-number statistics. The fit yields a mean site occu- pancy ¯n = 1.5(5). b, Time-resolved detector counts (per 10 ns bin) accumulated while transporting the first 10 lattice sites. The transmission peak at t = tp (z = zp) is clearly visible in the average over a 0.6 µs running window (solid line). c, Averaged transmission of four conveyor-belt transports (color symbols), demonstrating excellent alignment using the 10-site average as shown in b to...

FIG. 2. Simulated atom-photon coupling during trans- port. a, Potential cross-sections U(y, z) overlaid with a rep- resentative atomic trajectory (white lines, plotted from the initial time to the indicated time), where kB is the Boltz- mann constant. The marked atomic positions (red circles) follow the occupied lattice site (marked by dashed lines), as the distance of the site center to the surface (z0) moves from z0/d =1 (i) to 0.75 (ii), 0.5 (iii), 0.25 (iv), and 0 (v). b, Cal- culated resonant transmission T1 (gray line) and single-atom cooperativity C (red dashed line) during the transport, both averaged over 10 single-atom trajectories. c, Transmission Tn for n = 1, 2, . . . , 6 atoms...

FIG. 4. Near-deterministic single-atom loading. a, Schematic of feedback-controlled conveyor-belt transport. An FPGA monitors the time-averaged counts from the avalanche photodiode (APD) and triggers the arbitrary waveform generator (AWG) to halt the transport by ramping down ∆ν while ramping up the tweezer power. b, Probability histogram of the APD counts in 10 µs bins.
